source.title: All-Optical Electrophysiology Reveals the Role of Lateral Inhibition in Sensory Processing in Cortical Layer 1.

confidence_rationale: Direct in-vivo evidence that cholinergic input drives a winner-takes-all dynamic in cortical layer 1 interneurons, gating sensory processing through lateral inhibition. Layer 1 is the canonical recipient of long-range modulatory input including acetylcholine, and L1 interneurons project widely within the local cortical column. Fan et al. show with all-optical electrophysiology that aversive stimuli activate cholinergic input which evokes a bimodal (winner-take-all) spiking distribution in L1, and that the network functions as a temporal high-pass filter. Direct support for Q-039's hypothesis that acetylcholine gates the temporal integration window in cortex. Confidence at 0.78 because this is exactly the kind of mechanistic, causal evidence Q-039 needs (cholinergic causal manipulation + circuit-level temporal-filtering measurement), but the experiment is in mouse barrel cortex and the L1 winner-take-all dynamic is demonstrated, not the integration-window gating per se -- temporal-window inference is from the high-pass filter property.
failure_signatures: The temporal high-pass filter behaviour is a circuit property that emerges from lateral inhibition; it is not directly tunable by ACh independent of the inhibitory machinery. If REE's TCL needs an ACh-tunable integration window decoupled from the L1/inhibition circuit, this paper does not directly support that.
The experiment is in mouse barrel cortex (sensory). Whether L1 winner-take-all generalises to associative/prefrontal cortex (more relevant to REE's E3) is not tested here.

mapping.source_claim_statement: All-optical electrophysiology in mouse barrel cortex layer 1 (L1) demonstrates that whisker stimuli evoke precisely-timed single spikes in L1 interneurons followed by strong lateral inhibition. Aversive stimuli activate cholinergic input which evokes a bimodal (winner-takes-all) spiking distribution in L1. A simple conductance-based model containing only lateral inhibition within L1 recapitulates both sensory and cholinergic responses, and predicts that the network functions as a spatial and temporal high-pass filter for excitatory inputs. ACh-driven L1 dynamics gate sensory processing in underlying cortex.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-039 asks which neuromodulators implement the control-plane parameters of TCL (alpha_S, alpha_A, tau_E2, etc.) and specifically whether acetylcholine gates the temporal integration window. Fan et al. provide direct mechanistic evidence that cholinergic input to cortical L1 produces winner-take-all interneuron dynamics that act as a temporal high-pass filter on excitatory inputs to underlying cortex. This is exactly the kind of circuit ACh would use to set tau_E2-like integration windows: the high-pass filter property determines what counts as 'recent' for downstream cortex. For REE, this maps onto the architectural role of acetylcholine in setting alpha_A or tau_E2 in the TCL substrate. The L1 hub identified by Fan et al. is a plausible biological substrate for ARC-053's control plane.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper shows ACh-driven WTA in L1 and a temporal high-pass filter property; it does not directly demonstrate that ACh tunes the cut-off of that filter. The high-pass filter is an emergent circuit property. Whether ACh tunes the window or merely toggles between filtering and non-filtering modes is an open question this paper does not resolve. Additionally, mouse barrel cortex is a sensory area; REE's E3 sits closer to prefrontal/associative cortex where the L1 organisation is similar but the modulator-receptor expression patterns differ. The transfer is plausible but not direct.
mapping.source_context: Harvard Chemistry/MIT Media Lab/Mass Eye and Ear collaboration using novel all-optical electrophysiology methods. Cell paper, top-tier methods development.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high (Cell, methodological breakthrough, multi-PI top-tier collaboration). Mapping fidelity good -- the specific circuit mechanism (ACh gating L1 inhibition gating temporal filtering) maps directly onto Q-039's question about ACh and integration windows. Transfer risk moderate -- mouse barrel cortex to human prefrontal cortex is a substantial leap, and the paper does not directly demonstrate window-cutoff tuning.
